title: Vector component meaning
content: A vector component is one number inside a vector that describes movement, size, or direction along one axis. For example, a two-dimensional vector can have an x component and a y component. Components make vectors easier to calculate, compare, and store in programs.

title: Vector magnitude basic
content: Vector magnitude is the length or size of a vector. In geometry, it tells how far the vector reaches from its start to its end. Magnitude is useful in physics, graphics, and machine learning because many calculations need a clear measure of vector size.

title: Dot product basic
content: The dot product combines two vectors and returns one number. It is often used to measure how similar two directions are. If two vectors point in a similar direction, the dot product is larger; if they are at right angles, it becomes zero.

title: Matrix multiplication basic
content: Matrix multiplication is a way to combine two matrices by matching rows from the first matrix with columns from the second. The order matters, so A times B may not equal B times A. It is important in graphics, linear systems, and neural networks.

title: Identity matrix basic
content: An identity matrix is a square matrix that acts like the number one for matrix multiplication. It has ones on the main diagonal and zeros elsewhere. Multiplying a compatible matrix by the identity matrix keeps the original matrix unchanged.

title: Inverse matrix basic
content: An inverse matrix reverses the effect of another square matrix when it exists. If matrix A has an inverse, multiplying A by its inverse gives the identity matrix. Inverses are useful for solving linear equations, but not every matrix has one.

title: System of linear equations
content: A system of linear equations is a group of equations that must be true at the same time. The solution is the value or values that satisfy every equation together. These systems appear in modeling, engineering, economics, and computer graphics.

title: Basis vector basic
content: A basis is a set of vectors that can build every vector in a space by scaling and adding. Basis vectors give a coordinate system for describing points and directions. Changing the basis can make some problems easier to understand or compute.

title: Linear transformation basic
content: A linear transformation changes vectors while preserving addition and scaling rules. Many linear transformations can be represented by matrices. Rotations, scaling, projections, and shearing in graphics are common examples of linear transformations.

title: Eigenvalue basic
content: An eigenvalue is a number linked to a vector that keeps its direction after a linear transformation. The vector may become longer, shorter, or reversed, but it stays on the same line. Eigenvalues help analyze systems, data, and some machine learning methods.

title: Function input and output
content: A function connects each allowed input to exactly one output. In programming and math, this idea helps describe predictable behavior. A function can be shown by a formula, a graph, a table, or code that returns a result.

title: Domain and range basic
content: The domain of a function is the set of inputs it accepts. The range is the set of possible outputs it can produce. Knowing domain and range helps avoid invalid values, such as dividing by zero or taking a square root of a negative number in real numbers.

title: Slope as rate of change
content: Slope describes how much one value changes when another value changes. On a line, it is the rise divided by the run. In real examples, slope can represent speed, growth, cost per item, or any constant rate of change.

title: Derivative as instant rate
content: A derivative measures how fast a function is changing at a specific point. It can be understood as the slope of a curve at that point. Derivatives are used in motion, optimization, machine learning training, and many science models.

title: Positive and negative derivatives
content: A positive derivative usually means a function is increasing at that point, while a negative derivative means it is decreasing. A zero derivative can mark a flat point. This idea helps locate peaks, valleys, and turning points on graphs.

title: Integral as accumulation
content: An integral measures accumulated quantity over an interval. On a graph, it can represent the area under a curve. Integrals are useful for distance from speed, total cost from a changing rate, and many physics or statistics problems.

title: Limit basic intuition
content: A limit describes what value a function approaches as the input gets close to a point. The function does not always need to equal that value at the point. Limits are the foundation for derivatives, integrals, and careful reasoning about curves.

title: Chain rule basic
content: The chain rule is used to differentiate a function inside another function. It says that the outside change and the inside change both matter. This rule is important in calculus and also appears in neural network training through backpropagation.

title: Gradient basic
content: A gradient is a vector of partial derivatives. It points in the direction where a function increases fastest. In machine learning, gradients help adjust model parameters so the training loss becomes smaller over many update steps.

title: Sequence basic
content: A sequence is an ordered list of numbers or objects. Each item has a position, and many sequences follow a rule. Sequences appear in patterns, algorithms, time-series data, and mathematical series used to approximate values.

title: Series basic
content: A series is the sum of terms in a sequence. Some series end after a fixed number of terms, while others continue without end. Series help approximate functions, calculate totals, and study whether repeated additions stay finite or grow forever.

title: Probability event basic
content: An event in probability is something that can happen in an experiment or situation. Rolling an even number on a die is an event. Events help describe uncertain outcomes clearly, so we can calculate chances and compare possible results.

title: Conditional probability basic
content: Conditional probability measures the chance of one event when another event is already known. For example, it can ask the chance of rain given that the sky is cloudy. This idea is important in statistics, diagnosis, spam filters, and machine learning.

title: Independent events basic
content: Two events are independent when knowing one event happened does not change the chance of the other. For example, two fair coin flips are usually modeled as independent. Independence is a useful assumption, but it should not be used when events clearly influence each other.

title: Expected value basic
content: Expected value is the long-run average result of a random process. It does not always have to be a result that can happen in one try. Expected value is useful for games, risk, decisions, and understanding model predictions.

title: Random variable basic
content: A random variable assigns a number to outcomes of a random situation. For example, it can count how many heads appear after flipping coins. Random variables let probability problems be written with numbers, formulas, tables, and distributions.

title: Normal distribution basic
content: The normal distribution is a common bell-shaped distribution used to model many natural and measurement-based values. Its center is described by the mean, and its spread is described by the standard deviation. It is useful but not correct for every dataset.

title: Variance as data spread
content: Variance measures how spread out data values are from their mean. A small variance means values stay close to the average, while a large variance means they vary more. Variance is widely used in statistics, probability, and machine learning evaluation.

title: Standard deviation basic
content: Standard deviation measures typical distance from the mean in the same unit as the data. It is easier to interpret than variance because it matches the original measurement scale. A larger standard deviation usually means values are more spread out.

title: Correlation basic
content: Correlation measures how two variables tend to move together. Positive correlation means they often increase together, while negative correlation means one tends to decrease when the other increases. Correlation does not prove that one variable causes the other.

title: Median versus mean
content: The mean is the arithmetic average, while the median is the middle value after sorting data. The median is often more stable when extreme values are present. Choosing mean or median depends on the question and the shape of the dataset.

title: Histogram basic
content: A histogram groups numeric data into intervals and shows how many values fall in each interval. It helps reveal the shape of a dataset, such as skew, spread, or common ranges. Histograms are useful before choosing a model or summary statistic.

title: Regression basic
content: Regression is a method for modeling the relationship between variables. A simple regression can estimate how one value changes when another value changes. In machine learning, regression often means predicting a numeric output rather than a category.

title: Logarithm basic
content: A logarithm answers the question of what exponent is needed to get a number from a base. For example, base ten logarithms relate to powers of ten. Logarithms help describe growth, scales, information, and some machine learning loss functions.

title: Exponential growth basic
content: Exponential growth happens when a quantity grows by a constant factor over equal steps. It can start slowly and then rise quickly. This pattern appears in compound interest, population models, algorithms, and some early-stage spreading processes.

title: Mathematical model assumption
content: A mathematical model uses symbols, equations, or rules to represent part of reality. Every model makes assumptions, so it can be useful without being perfect. Good answers should explain what the model includes, what it ignores, and when it may fail.

title: Position velocity and acceleration
content: Position describes where an object is, velocity describes how its position changes, and acceleration describes how velocity changes. These three ideas form a basic language for motion. They are used for cars, falling objects, projectiles, and simulations.
aliases: vị trí vận tốc gia tốc

title: Net force basic
content: Net force is the total force acting on an object after combining all pushes and pulls. If forces cancel, the net force can be zero. A nonzero net force can change an object's motion by speeding it up, slowing it down, or changing direction.

title: Free body diagram
content: A free body diagram is a simple drawing that shows the forces acting on one object. It usually replaces the object with a dot or box and uses arrows for forces. This helps students solve motion problems without mixing up different objects.

title: Inertia basic
content: Inertia is the tendency of an object to keep its current state of motion. An object at rest tends to stay at rest, and a moving object tends to keep moving in a straight line unless a net force acts on it. Mass measures inertia.

title: Momentum basic
content: Momentum depends on an object's mass and velocity. A heavy moving object or a fast object can have large momentum. Momentum is useful for understanding collisions, recoil, and why stopping a moving object can require a strong force or more time.

title: Impulse basic
content: Impulse is the effect of a force applied over time. It changes an object's momentum. In safety design, increasing the stopping time can reduce the average force, which is why airbags and cushions help during sudden impacts.

title: Work and energy basic
content: In physics, work happens when a force moves an object over a distance in the force direction. Work can transfer energy into or out of a system. This idea connects pushing, lifting, friction, and motion in a clear mathematical way.

title: Kinetic energy basic
content: Kinetic energy is the energy an object has because it is moving. Faster objects have more kinetic energy, and heavier objects also have more at the same speed. It helps explain crashes, projectiles, machines, and many motion problems.

title: Potential energy basic
content: Potential energy is stored energy related to position, shape, or configuration. A lifted object has gravitational potential energy, while a stretched spring has elastic potential energy. Potential energy can change into kinetic energy when the system is allowed to move.

title: Conservation of energy
content: Conservation of energy means energy is not created or destroyed in an isolated system; it changes form. For example, gravitational potential energy can become kinetic energy during a fall. Real systems may also transform energy into heat, sound, or deformation.

title: Friction basic
content: Friction is a force that resists relative motion between surfaces. It can slow objects down, but it also allows walking, driving, and gripping. Friction depends on the surfaces and the normal force, not only on how large the object looks.

title: Gravity basic
content: Gravity is an attractive interaction between masses. Near Earth, it pulls objects downward and gives them weight. Gravity explains falling objects, orbits, tides, and large-scale structures, although different theories describe it at different levels of detail.

title: Weight versus mass
content: Mass measures how much matter or inertia an object has, while weight is the gravitational force on that mass. Mass stays the same in many locations, but weight can change on the Moon or another planet because gravity is different.

title: Pressure basic
content: Pressure is force applied over an area. The same force creates higher pressure when it is spread over a smaller area. This explains sharp knives, snowshoes, hydraulic systems, and why fluids can push on surfaces in every direction.

title: Density basic
content: Density tells how much mass is packed into a given volume. A material with high density has more mass in the same space. Density helps explain floating, sinking, material choice, and why some objects feel heavier than others of the same size.

title: Wave frequency basic
content: Frequency is how many wave cycles pass a point each second. Higher frequency often means a higher sound pitch or different electromagnetic color. Frequency is linked with period, wavelength, and wave speed in many wave problems.

title: Wavelength basic
content: Wavelength is the distance between matching points on a wave, such as crest to crest. Shorter wavelengths often correspond to higher frequencies when wave speed is fixed. Wavelength matters in sound, light, radio communication, and quantum physics.

title: Amplitude basic
content: Amplitude describes the size of a wave's disturbance from its rest position. In sound, larger amplitude often relates to louder sound. In other waves, amplitude can relate to energy, signal strength, or intensity depending on the physical system.

title: Resonance basic
content: Resonance happens when a system is driven near a natural frequency and responds strongly. A swing moves higher when pushed at the right rhythm. Resonance appears in music, bridges, circuits, and many mechanical or wave systems.

title: Electric charge basic
content: Electric charge is a property of matter that can cause electric forces. Charges can be positive or negative, and like charges repel while opposite charges attract. Charge is central to electricity, circuits, atoms, and electromagnetic interactions.

title: Voltage basic
content: Voltage is electric potential difference between two points. It can be understood as the push that encourages electric charge to move through a circuit. Voltage alone does not describe a circuit fully; current and resistance also matter.

title: Electric current basic
content: Electric current is the flow of electric charge. In a wire, it is often related to the movement of electrons, although conventional current direction is defined differently. Current is measured in amperes and depends on voltage, resistance, and circuit design.

title: Electrical resistance basic
content: Resistance describes how much a material or component opposes electric current. A high resistance reduces current for a given voltage. Resistors, wires, lamps, and sensors all have resistance, and heat can be produced when current passes through resistance.

title: Series and parallel circuits
content: In a series circuit, components share one path for current. In a parallel circuit, current can split through multiple paths. This difference affects brightness, resistance, and what happens if one component fails, making it important for basic circuit design.

title: Physics modeling simplification
content: Physics problems often use simplified models to focus on the most important effects. A model may ignore air resistance, friction, or object shape to make reasoning easier. The answer should mention assumptions because a simplified model is not the full real world.

title: Measurement uncertainty
content: Measurement uncertainty means a measured value is not perfectly exact. Tools have limits, and repeated measurements can vary. Scientists report uncertainty to show confidence and avoid pretending that a number is more precise than the evidence supports.

title: Physics simulation basic
content: A physics simulation uses equations and computer steps to estimate how a system changes over time. Simulations are useful when real experiments are expensive, dangerous, slow, or hard to repeat. They still need validation against real observations.

title: Machine learning surrogate model in physics
content: A surrogate model is a faster approximate model trained to imitate a slower physics simulation or calculation. It can help explore many cases quickly. However, it should be checked carefully because it may fail outside the data range used for training.

title: Machine learning for experiment patterns
content: Machine learning can help find patterns in experimental physics data, such as signals, clusters, or unusual events. It does not replace physical reasoning by itself. Scientists still need domain knowledge, careful validation, and uncertainty estimates before trusting results.

title: Units and dimensional checking
content: Checking units is a simple way to catch mistakes in physics equations. If the left and right sides of an equation have different dimensions, something is wrong. Unit reasoning helps students verify formulas before trusting a numerical answer.

title: AI agent basic concept
content: An AI agent is a system that observes information, makes decisions, and takes actions toward a goal. It can be simple, like a rule-based game bot, or complex, like a planning assistant. The key idea is interaction with an environment.

title: Symbolic AI basic
content: Symbolic AI represents knowledge using symbols, rules, and logic. It can be useful when rules are clear and explanations matter. It differs from many machine learning systems, which learn patterns from data instead of relying only on hand-written rules.

title: Rule based system
content: A rule based system uses if-then rules to make decisions. It can be easy to inspect and debug when the domain is small. However, it becomes hard to maintain when many exceptions, uncertain cases, or changing conditions appear.

title: Search problem in AI
content: In AI, a search problem asks how to find a path from a starting state to a goal state. The system explores possible actions and states. Search is used in games, planning, routing, puzzle solving, and decision-making tasks.

title: Heuristic basic
content: A heuristic is a practical rule or estimate that helps guide search or decision making. It may not guarantee the perfect answer, but it can make a problem much faster to solve. Good heuristics use useful domain knowledge.

title: Training data quality
content: Training data quality strongly affects a machine learning model. Wrong labels, duplicated data, missing examples, or biased samples can make the model unreliable. Better data cleaning, documentation, and evaluation usually improve results more than simply using a larger model.

title: Label in machine learning
content: A label is the target answer attached to a training example in supervised learning. For example, an image may have the label cat or dog. Labels teach the model what output it should learn, so noisy labels can hurt performance.

title: Feature in machine learning
content: A feature is an input value or property used by a machine learning model. In house price prediction, features can include area, location, and number of rooms. Good features make important patterns easier for the model to learn.

title: Target variable basic
content: The target variable is the value a model tries to predict. In classification, it may be a class label, and in regression, it may be a number. Separating inputs from the target helps organize datasets for training and evaluation.

title: Train test split basic
content: A train test split divides data into one part for learning and another part for evaluation. The test set should stay unseen during training. This helps estimate how well the model may work on new examples instead of only memorizing known data.

title: Validation set basic
content: A validation set is data used to tune choices during model development, such as parameters or early stopping. It is separate from the training set and should not replace a final test set. This reduces the risk of overfitting to evaluation decisions.

title: Cross validation basic
content: Cross validation evaluates a model by splitting data into several folds and testing on different folds. It gives a more stable estimate when the dataset is not very large. It is useful, but it can take more computation than one train test split.

title: Overfitting in machine learning
content: Overfitting happens when a machine learning model learns the training data too closely, including noise or accidental patterns. It may perform well on training examples but poorly on new data. More data, regularization, simpler models, and validation testing can reduce it.

title: Underfitting in machine learning
content: Underfitting happens when a model is too simple or poorly trained to learn important patterns in the data. It performs badly on both training data and new data. Fixes may include better features, a more suitable model, or longer training.

title: Bias variance tradeoff
content: The bias variance tradeoff describes two common sources of prediction error. High bias can mean the model is too simple, while high variance can mean it is too sensitive to training data. Good modeling tries to balance both problems.

title: Regularization basic
content: Regularization adds constraints or penalties to a learning process to discourage overly complex models. It can help reduce overfitting and improve generalization. The best regularization strength depends on the data, model, and evaluation method.

title: Loss function basic
content: A loss function measures how wrong a model's prediction is for a training example or batch. Training usually tries to reduce this loss over time. Different tasks need different losses, such as classification loss or numeric prediction loss.

title: Gradient descent basic
content: Gradient descent is an optimization method that changes model parameters in a direction that reduces loss. It uses gradients to decide how to update weights. The learning rate controls step size, and a poor value can slow or destabilize training.

title: Learning rate basic
content: The learning rate controls how large each training update is. A very small learning rate can make training slow, while a very large one can overshoot good solutions. Choosing it often requires testing, schedules, or adaptive optimizers.

title: Classification task basic
content: Classification is a machine learning task where the model predicts a category. Examples include spam or not spam, cat or dog, and positive or negative sentiment. Classification outputs should be evaluated with suitable metrics, not only with raw confidence.

title: Regression task in ML
content: Regression is a machine learning task where the model predicts a numeric value. Examples include house price, temperature, or demand prediction. A regression answer should not be confused with statistical regression theory, although the ideas are related.

title: Clustering basic
content: Clustering is an unsupervised learning method that groups similar data points without using labels. It can help explore customer groups, document topics, or image patterns. The groups are not always meaningful, so humans must interpret them carefully.

title: Dimensionality reduction basic
content: Dimensionality reduction reduces the number of features while keeping important information. It can help visualization, noise reduction, and faster training. However, it may remove useful details, so the reduced data should still be checked for the task.

title: Supervised learning detail
content: Supervised learning trains a model using examples that include inputs and correct target answers. The model learns a mapping from input to output. It is commonly used for classification, regression, and many practical prediction systems.

title: Unsupervised learning detail
content: Unsupervised learning looks for structure in data without target labels. It can discover clusters, lower-dimensional patterns, or unusual points. Because no correct answer is provided, results often need human interpretation and domain knowledge.

title: Reinforcement learning basic
content: Reinforcement learning trains an agent to choose actions by receiving rewards or penalties from an environment. It is often used to study games, robotics, and control problems. It differs from supervised learning because feedback may be delayed.

title: Confusion matrix basic
content: A confusion matrix summarizes classification results by counting correct and incorrect predictions for each class. It helps show which categories the model confuses. This is more informative than a single accuracy number, especially for imbalanced datasets.

title: Precision and recall basic
content: Precision measures how many predicted positives are actually correct, while recall measures how many real positives are found. They are useful when false positives and false negatives have different costs. Spam detection and medical screening often need this distinction.
aliases: độ chính xác và độ phủ

title: Accuracy limitation
content: Accuracy is the fraction of predictions that are correct, but it can be misleading when classes are imbalanced. A model can score high by guessing the majority class. Good evaluation should consider the task, data balance, and error costs.

title: Data leakage basic
content: Data leakage happens when training uses information that would not be available at prediction time. It can make evaluation look much better than real performance. Leakage may come from duplicated records, wrong split timing, or features that secretly contain the answer.

title: Activation function basic
content: An activation function adds nonlinearity to a neural network. Without nonlinear activations, many layers may behave like a simple linear model. Common activations help networks learn complex patterns, but the best choice depends on the architecture and task.

title: Backpropagation basic
content: Backpropagation is a method for computing how each neural network weight contributes to the loss. It applies the chain rule from calculus through network layers. Training then uses these gradients to update weights and reduce errors over many examples.

title: Neural network layer
content: A neural network layer transforms input values into new values using weights, biases, and often an activation function. Stacking layers lets a model build more abstract representations. Different layer types are used for text, images, audio, or general data.

title: Weights and biases in neural networks
content: Weights and biases are trainable numbers inside a neural network. Weights control how strongly inputs affect outputs, while biases shift the result. During training, the model updates these values to reduce prediction errors on training data.

title: Epoch and batch basic
content: An epoch means one full pass through the training dataset. A batch is a smaller group of examples processed before one update step. Batch training is practical because modern datasets are large and updating after every single example can be inefficient.

title: Embedding basic
content: An embedding represents an item, such as a word or document, as a vector of numbers. Similar items often have vectors that are close in the embedding space. Embeddings are useful for search, recommendation, language models, and RAG systems.

title: Token in NLP
content: A token is a small unit of text used by a language model or NLP system. It may be a word, part of a word, punctuation mark, or symbol. Tokenization affects context length, cost, and how text is processed.

title: Tokenization basic
content: Tokenization splits text into tokens before the model processes it. Different tokenizers can split the same sentence in different ways. This matters because a model only sees tokens, not raw human text exactly as people read it.

title: Sentiment analysis basic
content: Sentiment analysis tries to identify attitude or emotion in text, such as positive, negative, or neutral. It can be useful for reviews and feedback. Results can be wrong when sarcasm, context, mixed feelings, or slang are involved.

title: Named entity recognition basic
content: Named entity recognition finds names of people, places, organizations, dates, or other special items in text. It helps structure unorganized documents. Mistakes can happen when names are ambiguous, misspelled, or used in unusual contexts.

title: Image classification basic
content: Image classification predicts the main category of an image. A model might classify an image as cat, car, or building. It does not necessarily locate every object or explain the scene, so it is different from object detection.

title: Object detection basic
content: Object detection identifies objects in an image and usually marks their locations with boxes. It is more detailed than image classification because it asks what is present and where it is. It is used in cameras, robotics, and visual search.

title: Image segmentation basic
content: Image segmentation divides an image into meaningful regions, often at the pixel level. It can separate roads, people, sky, or organs in medical images. Segmentation is useful when exact shape and area matter more than a simple label.

title: LLM context window
content: A context window is the amount of text a language model can consider at one time. Text outside the window may be unavailable to the model during generation. RAG systems help by retrieving relevant documents and placing them inside the prompt.

title: LLM hallucination basic
content: An LLM hallucination is an answer that sounds confident but is false, unsupported, or invented. It can happen because the model predicts likely text rather than checking truth directly. Retrieval, citations, testing, and cautious wording can reduce this risk.

title: Prompt instruction basic
content: A prompt instruction tells a language model what task to perform, what style to use, and what constraints to follow. Clear prompts can improve consistency, but they cannot guarantee perfect reasoning or factual accuracy. Testing is still needed.

title: LLM temperature basic
content: Temperature is a generation setting that affects randomness in model outputs. Lower values usually make answers more focused and repeatable, while higher values can make them more varied. It does not improve factual knowledge by itself.

title: RAG chunk basic
content: A RAG chunk is a small piece of text stored for retrieval. Smaller chunks can be more focused, while larger chunks keep more context together. Good chunking helps the model find relevant facts without flooding the prompt with unrelated information.

title: Embedding search in RAG
content: Embedding search converts a user question and documents into vectors, then compares them to find related text. It is useful when exact keywords are missing. However, it can retrieve text that is semantically close but not actually correct for the question.

title: Generative AI text output
content: Generative AI can create text, images, audio, or code based on patterns learned from data. For text generation, the system predicts likely next tokens. Generated output should be reviewed because it may contain mistakes, bias, or unsupported claims.

title: Generative AI image output
content: Generative AI image systems create pictures from prompts, references, or other inputs. They can help with design and ideation, but outputs may contain artifacts or inaccurate details. Users should be careful with copyrighted styles, real people, and misleading images.

title: AI assisted code review
content: AI-assisted software development can help explain code, suggest fixes, and generate drafts. It should not replace human review, tests, or security checks. A useful workflow asks the AI for reasoning, then verifies behavior by running the code.

title: AI assisted debugging
content: AI can help debugging by reading errors, explaining likely causes, and suggesting experiments. The user should provide the exact error, relevant code, and environment details. Good debugging still requires testing one change at a time and confirming the real cause.

title: AI model limitations
content: AI models have limits because they learn from data, patterns, and design choices. They can be wrong, biased, outdated, or overconfident. A safe chatbot should admit uncertainty, ask for missing context when needed, and avoid pretending to know private facts.

title: Evaluation dataset basic
content: An evaluation dataset is used to measure how well a model performs on examples not used for training. It should match the real task as much as possible. Poor evaluation data can make a weak model look strong or a useful model look bad.

title: Inference in machine learning
content: Inference is the stage where a trained model receives new input and produces an output. It is different from training because the model usually does not update its weights. Inference speed, cost, and reliability matter for real applications.

title: Model deployment basic
content: Model deployment means putting a trained model into an application or service so users can send inputs and receive predictions. Deployment must consider latency, memory, monitoring, security, and fallback behavior. A working notebook is not the same as a reliable product.

title: Variable basic
content: A variable is a named place in a program that stores a value. The value can be used later, changed, or passed into functions. Good variable names make code easier to read, debug, and explain to other programmers.

title: Control flow basic
content: Control flow is the order in which program instructions run. Conditions, loops, and function calls can change that order. Understanding control flow helps programmers predict behavior, find bugs, and avoid code that runs at the wrong time.

title: If else statement basic
content: An if else statement lets a program choose between different paths. If a condition is true, one block runs; otherwise, another block can run. This is a basic tool for decisions such as login checks, input validation, and game logic.

title: Loop basic
content: A loop repeats a block of code while a condition is true or for each item in a collection. Loops reduce repeated code and help process arrays, files, and database results. A loop needs a safe stopping condition to avoid running forever.

title: Function parameter and return
content: A function can receive inputs called parameters and send back a result using a return value. Parameters make the function flexible, while return values let other code use the result. This pattern helps split a program into reusable pieces.

title: Error handling basic
content: Error handling is the practice of responding to problems without crashing the whole program. Code can catch exceptions, return useful messages, or log details for debugging. Good error handling helps users understand what went wrong and helps developers fix it.

title: API endpoint basic
content: An API endpoint is a specific URL or route where a client can send a request to a server. Each endpoint usually has a purpose, such as login, search, or saving data. Clear endpoint names make frontend and backend integration easier.

title: HTTP method basic
content: HTTP methods describe the intended action of a web request. GET often reads data, POST creates data, PUT or PATCH updates data, and DELETE removes data. APIs should use methods consistently so clients understand the server's behavior.

title: Client server model
content: In the client server model, a client sends requests and a server sends responses. A React app can be the client, while Node.js or FastAPI can act as the server. This separation helps organize user interface, business logic, and data access.

title: Environment variable basic
content: An environment variable stores configuration outside the source code. It can hold database URLs, API keys, ports, or mode settings. Keeping configuration separate helps run the same program in development, testing, and production with different values.

title: Python function basic
content: A Python function is a reusable block of code that can take inputs and return an output. Functions help organize programs, reduce repeated code, and make behavior easier to test. A simple function uses def, a name, optional parameters, and an indented body.

title: Python indentation basic
content: Python uses indentation to show which lines belong inside a block, such as a function, loop, or if statement. Wrong indentation can change program meaning or cause an error. Consistent spacing is therefore important in Python code.

title: Python list comprehension basic
content: A Python list comprehension creates a new list using a compact expression. It is often used to transform or filter items from another iterable. It can make simple code shorter, but complex comprehensions may be harder to read than normal loops.

title: Python exception basic
content: A Python exception is an error event that interrupts normal program flow. Code can handle exceptions with try and except blocks. Handling exceptions is useful for file errors, invalid input, network problems, and database failures.

title: Python virtual environment
content: A Python virtual environment is an isolated folder for project dependencies. It helps prevent package versions from different projects from conflicting. For AI or FastAPI projects, using a virtual environment makes setup cleaner and easier to reproduce.

title: Python package and pip
content: A Python package is reusable code that can be installed into a project. Pip is a common tool for installing packages from package indexes or local files. Pinning versions can help avoid unexpected behavior when dependencies change.

title: FastAPI route concept
content: A FastAPI route connects a URL path and HTTP method to a Python function. When a request reaches that route, FastAPI calls the function and returns a response. This structure is useful for building APIs around search, RAG, or model inference.

title: JavaScript let and const
content: In JavaScript, let and const declare block-scoped variables. Let allows reassignment, while const prevents reassignment of the variable binding. Const does not always make an object deeply immutable, so object properties can still be changed unless protected.

title: JavaScript object basic
content: A JavaScript object stores related data as key value pairs. Objects are common for user profiles, API responses, configuration, and application state. They are similar to JSON objects in shape, but JavaScript objects can include functions and non-JSON values.

title: JavaScript array methods
content: JavaScript arrays have methods such as map, filter, and reduce for processing lists of values. These methods are common in React and data transformation. They can make code cleaner when used simply, but overusing them can make logic hard to follow.

title: JavaScript Promise basic
content: A JavaScript Promise represents a value that may be available now, later, or never because of an error. Promises are often used for network requests and async tasks. Code can handle results with then and catch or with async and await.

title: JavaScript async await detail
content: Async and await make promise-based JavaScript code look closer to normal step-by-step code. An async function returns a promise, and await pauses inside that function until the promise settles. Error handling still needs try and catch in many cases.

title: JavaScript event loop
content: The JavaScript event loop helps handle asynchronous work without blocking the main thread forever. It coordinates the call stack, task queues, and callbacks. This is why timers, network requests, and UI events can complete after current code finishes.

title: Node.js event loop
content: The Node.js event loop lets Node handle many I/O tasks without creating a new thread for every request. It is useful for web servers, file operations, and network communication. CPU-heavy work can still block the event loop if not handled carefully.

title: NPM basic
content: NPM is a package manager commonly used with Node.js projects. It installs libraries, manages scripts, and records dependencies in package files. Keeping dependencies clear helps other developers install and run the project consistently.

title: package.json basic
content: The package.json file describes a Node.js project. It can include scripts, dependencies, project metadata, and version requirements. In a React or Node backend project, package.json helps define how to install, run, build, and test the application.

title: Express middleware basic
content: Express middleware is code that runs during the request response cycle before the final route response. Middleware can parse JSON, check authentication, log requests, or handle errors. The order of middleware matters because requests pass through it step by step.

title: Algorithm complexity basic
content: Algorithm complexity describes how time or memory use grows as input size grows. It helps compare approaches without measuring only one machine. Big O notation gives a simplified view of growth, such as constant, linear, or quadratic behavior.

title: Binary search basic
content: Binary search finds a target in a sorted list by repeatedly checking the middle and discarding half of the remaining search area. It is much faster than checking every item for large sorted data. It only works correctly when the data is ordered.

title: Sorting algorithm basic
content: A sorting algorithm rearranges items into an order, such as smallest to largest or alphabetical order. Different sorting methods have different speed, memory use, and stability. Choosing a sort can matter when data is large or order ties must be preserved.

title: Stack and queue basic
content: A stack removes the newest item first, like a pile of plates. A queue removes the oldest item first, like a waiting line. These structures are useful for undo actions, parsing, scheduling, breadth-first search, and task processing.

title: Hash table basic
content: A hash table stores key value pairs and uses a hash function to find where a key belongs. It can provide very fast lookup on average. Dictionaries in many languages use hash-table ideas, but collisions and memory use still matter.

title: Tree data structure basic
content: A tree is a data structure made of nodes connected by parent child relationships. It is useful for representing folders, menus, syntax, and search structures. A tree has a root node, and nodes without children are often called leaves.

title: Graph data structure basic
content: A graph is a structure of nodes connected by edges. It can represent maps, social networks, dependencies, or routes. Graphs can be directed or undirected, weighted or unweighted, and they often need special traversal algorithms.

title: Git repository basic
content: A Git repository stores project files and their version history. It lets developers track changes, compare older versions, and collaborate safely. A repository can exist locally on a computer and remotely on a service used for sharing.

title: Git commit basic
content: A Git commit is a saved snapshot of changes in a repository. Each commit has an identifier and usually a message explaining what changed. Good commit messages help developers understand project history and undo or inspect changes later.

title: Git branch basic
content: A Git branch is a separate line of development. Developers use branches to work on features, fixes, or experiments without immediately changing the main branch. Branches make collaboration safer, but they need merging or rebasing to combine work.

title: Git merge conflict basic
content: A merge conflict happens when Git cannot automatically combine changes from different branches. The developer must choose or edit the final content. Conflicts are normal in collaboration and should be resolved carefully with testing afterward.

title: Flutter widget basic
content: In Flutter, a widget describes part of the user interface. Buttons, text, layouts, and entire screens can be represented as widgets. Flutter builds UI by composing widgets together, which makes interface code structured and reusable.

title: Dart null safety basic
content: Dart null safety helps prevent errors caused by using null where a real value is required. Types are non-nullable by default unless marked as nullable. This makes many mistakes easier to catch before the app runs.

title: JSON syntax parse error
content: A JSON parse error happens when text is not valid JSON syntax. Common causes include trailing commas, comments, missing quotes around keys, or using single quotes instead of double quotes. Checking the exact error position usually helps find the broken character.

title: JSON string number boolean
content: JSON supports simple value types such as strings, numbers, booleans, and null. Strings must use double quotes, numbers are written without quotes, and booleans are true or false. Using the wrong type can break validation or application logic.

title: Nested JSON object
content: A nested JSON object is an object inside another object. It helps represent structured data, such as a user with a profile and settings. Deep nesting can become hard to query, update, and validate, so structure should match the app's needs.

title: JSON array of objects
content: A JSON array of objects stores a list where each item is an object with fields. It is common for search results, knowledge records, and API responses. Keeping object fields consistent makes the data easier to validate and import into databases.

title: JSON schema validation
content: JSON schema validation checks whether JSON data follows expected fields, types, and rules. It helps catch missing properties, wrong value types, and invalid formats before storing or using data. Validation is useful for APIs, configs, and RAG knowledge files.

title: Primary key basic
content: A primary key uniquely identifies each row in a relational table. It should not be duplicated, and it is usually stable for the life of the record. Primary keys help connect tables and make updates or lookups safer.

title: Foreign key basic
content: A foreign key is a field that points to a primary key in another table. It helps represent relationships, such as orders belonging to users. Foreign keys can protect data consistency by preventing references to records that do not exist.

title: Table row and column
content: In a relational database, a table stores data in rows and columns. A row represents one record, while a column represents one kind of value. This structure is useful when data has a clear schema and relationships between entities.

title: Database normalization basic
content: Database normalization organizes relational data to reduce duplication and improve consistency. It often separates repeated information into related tables. Normalization can make updates safer, but overly complex schemas may require more joins and careful query design.

title: Database denormalization basic
content: Denormalization intentionally stores duplicated or combined data to make some reads faster or simpler. It can improve performance for certain workloads, but it increases the risk of inconsistent data. The decision should be based on real query needs.

title: SQL SELECT WHERE basic
content: A SQL SELECT query reads data from a table, and a WHERE clause filters which rows are returned. This lets an application request only relevant records. Good filters reduce unnecessary data transfer and can use indexes when designed well.

title: SQL JOIN basic
content: A SQL JOIN combines rows from two or more tables based on related columns. Joins are useful when data is normalized across separate tables. Common types include inner join and left join, and choosing the wrong join can change results.

title: SQL GROUP BY basic
content: SQL GROUP BY collects rows that share a value and allows aggregate calculations, such as count, sum, or average. It is useful for reports and summaries. The selected columns must match the grouping logic or use aggregate functions.

title: SQL ORDER BY and LIMIT
content: ORDER BY sorts SQL query results by one or more columns. LIMIT restricts how many rows are returned. Together they are useful for showing newest posts, highest scores, or the first page of search results.

title: SQL index basic
content: A SQL index is a data structure that helps the database find rows faster for certain queries. Indexes can speed up reads but may slow down writes because the index also needs updating. Useful indexes match frequent filters, joins, or sorting patterns.

title: SQL injection basic
content: SQL injection is a security problem where unsafe user input changes the meaning of a SQL query. It can expose, change, or delete data. Parameterized queries and careful input handling are common ways to reduce this risk.

title: ACID properties in transactions
content: ACID describes important properties of reliable database transactions: atomicity, consistency, isolation, and durability. These ideas help ensure that multi-step database operations behave safely. For example, a payment should not remove money from one account without adding it to the other account.

title: Commit and rollback basic
content: Commit saves a transaction's changes permanently, while rollback cancels the changes made by a transaction. These actions help keep data safe when a multi-step operation fails. They are especially important for payments, inventory, and account updates.

title: Transaction isolation basic
content: Transaction isolation controls how much one transaction can see changes from another transaction before they finish. Stronger isolation can prevent confusing results but may reduce performance. The right level depends on correctness needs and workload.

title: MongoDB collection and document
content: In MongoDB, a collection stores documents, and each document is a flexible record often written in a JSON-like format. A collection is similar to a table in purpose, but documents can have different fields when the schema is flexible.

title: BSON in MongoDB
content: MongoDB stores data in BSON, a binary format related to JSON. BSON supports additional data types and efficient storage for database operations. Developers often write data in JSON-like syntax, but MongoDB internally stores and transfers it as BSON.

title: MongoDB ObjectId basic
content: MongoDB commonly uses ObjectId values as unique identifiers for documents. An ObjectId is not the same as a normal string, although it is often displayed as text. Applications should handle it consistently when querying, inserting, or serializing data.

title: MongoDB find query basic
content: A MongoDB find query searches documents in a collection using filter conditions. For example, it can match a field value or compare numbers. Clear filters help return only relevant documents and avoid scanning more data than necessary.

title: MongoDB update operators
content: MongoDB update operators change specific parts of a document without replacing the whole document. Operators such as set or increment are common for editing fields and counters. Using targeted updates can reduce mistakes and keep unrelated fields unchanged.

title: MongoDB index basic
content: A MongoDB index helps the database find matching documents faster for certain queries. Indexes are useful for fields used often in filters or sorting. Too many indexes can increase storage use and make writes slower, so they should match real access patterns.

title: MongoDB aggregation pipeline
content: The MongoDB aggregation pipeline processes documents through stages, such as filtering, grouping, sorting, or reshaping. It is useful for reports and transformations inside the database. Complex pipelines should be tested carefully because they can become slow or hard to maintain.

title: MongoDB schema flexibility
content: MongoDB documents can have flexible structures, so not every document in a collection must contain the same fields. This is useful for changing application data, but it does not mean schema design is unnecessary. Validation and consistent patterns still help.

title: Document database nested data
content: A document database can store nested data inside one record, such as a user with settings or profile details. This can reduce joins for some reads. However, deeply nested documents can be harder to update, index, and share across features.

title: Reference versus embed in document databases
content: In a document database, related data can be embedded inside one document or referenced by an id. Embedding can make reads simple, while references can avoid duplication. The better choice depends on update frequency, data size, and query patterns.

title: Database query plan basic
content: A query plan describes how a database intends to execute a query. It may show whether indexes are used, how tables or documents are scanned, and which operations are expensive. Reading query plans helps diagnose slow database performance.

title: Database connection pool
content: A connection pool keeps a set of database connections ready for reuse. This avoids opening a new connection for every request, which can be slow. Pool size should be configured carefully so the app does not overload the database.

title: Database backup basic
content: A database backup is a saved copy of data used for recovery after mistakes, corruption, or system failure. Backups should be tested, not only created. A backup strategy often considers frequency, storage location, retention, and restore time.

title: Database migration basic
content: A database migration changes schema or data in a controlled way, such as adding a field, creating an index, or changing a table. Migrations help teams apply changes consistently across development and production environments. Rollback planning is important.

title: Big data volume velocity variety
content: Big data is often described with volume, velocity, and variety. Volume means large size, velocity means fast data generation, and variety means many formats. These ideas explain why normal tools may become difficult to use at large scale.

title: Big data tool caution
content: Big data tools are useful when data size, speed, or distribution exceeds what simple tools can handle. A small chatbot project may not need Spark or a cluster at first. Starting simple can reduce complexity until real scale problems appear.

title: Apache Spark RDD basic
content: An RDD is an older core Spark abstraction for distributed collections of data. It supports transformations and actions across a cluster. Many modern Spark users work more often with DataFrames, but understanding RDDs helps explain Spark's distributed processing roots.

title: Apache Spark DataFrame basic
content: A Spark DataFrame represents structured data in rows and columns across distributed machines. It is similar in shape to a table, but designed for large-scale processing. DataFrames allow Spark to optimize queries and transformations more effectively than low-level operations.

title: Spark transformation and action
content: In Spark, transformations describe data processing steps, while actions trigger actual computation and return results or write output. This lazy evaluation lets Spark build an execution plan before running. Understanding this helps explain why some Spark code does nothing until an action.
aliases: biến đổi và hành động Spark

title: Spark cluster basic
content: A Spark cluster uses multiple machines or processes to handle large data tasks. Work is divided across executors under the control of a driver program. Clusters can process more data than one machine, but they also add setup and debugging complexity.

title: Data pipeline basic
content: A data pipeline moves and transforms data from one place to another. It may collect raw data, clean it, validate it, and load it into a database or search system. Reliable pipelines need logging, error handling, and repeatable steps.

title: ETL basic
content: ETL means extract, transform, and load. It describes a process where data is taken from sources, cleaned or reshaped, and stored in a target system. ETL is common in reporting, analytics, search indexes, and knowledge base preparation.

title: Data quality validation
content: Data quality validation checks whether data is complete, consistent, and usable before it enters an application or analysis. It can catch missing fields, wrong types, duplicates, and invalid values. Good validation reduces bugs in search, models, and reports.
aliases: kiểm tra chất lượng dữ liệu

title: Qubit basic
content: A qubit is the basic unit of information in quantum computing. Unlike a classical bit that is read as zero or one, a qubit is described by a quantum state before measurement. Measurement gives a classical result, so explanations should avoid saying it is simply both values forever.

title: Superposition basic
content: Superposition means a quantum state can be described as a combination of possible states before measurement. It is not the same as a normal object secretly choosing one value. Superposition is one reason quantum algorithms can behave differently from classical ones.

title: Entanglement basic
content: Entanglement is a quantum relationship where parts of a system are described together, even when separated. Measuring one part gives information linked to the other. Entanglement is important in quantum computing and communication, but it does not allow faster-than-light messaging.

title: Quantum measurement basic
content: Quantum measurement turns a quantum state into a classical outcome that can be recorded. The result is probabilistic, and the act of measurement changes what can be known about the system. This makes quantum information different from ordinary stored computer bits.

title: Quantum gate basic
content: A quantum gate is an operation that changes the state of one or more qubits. Quantum circuits are built from sequences of gates, somewhat like classical circuits use logic gates. The mathematics is different because gates must follow quantum rules.

title: Quantum circuit basic
content: A quantum circuit is a model for arranging quantum gates, qubits, and measurements in a planned order. It describes how a quantum algorithm is executed. The final measurements produce classical bits that people or normal computers can read.

title: Quantum algorithm basic
content: A quantum algorithm is a step-by-step method designed to run on a quantum computer. Some algorithms may offer advantages for specific tasks, such as certain searches or simulations. Quantum computers are not automatically faster for every problem.

title: Decoherence basic
content: Decoherence happens when a quantum system loses the delicate behavior needed for quantum computation because of unwanted interaction with its environment. It is a major challenge for building reliable quantum computers. Error correction and careful hardware design try to reduce its effects.

title: Quantum error correction basic
content: Quantum error correction protects quantum information from noise and errors without simply copying qubits in the classical way. It uses special encoding across multiple physical qubits. This is important because real quantum hardware is sensitive and imperfect.

title: Quantum communication basic
content: Quantum communication studies ways to use quantum states for transmitting or protecting information. Some methods are related to secure key distribution. It should not be confused with magical instant messaging, because known physics still limits usable communication.

title: Hybrid quantum classical computing
content: Many practical quantum workflows are hybrid, using a classical computer together with a quantum processor. The classical part prepares data, controls optimization, and reads results. This approach reflects current hardware limits and the need for ordinary computation around quantum steps.

title: Quantum computing beginner caution
content: Quantum computing is powerful for some specialized problems, but it is not a replacement for all normal computers. Many everyday tasks are better handled by classical hardware. A beginner-friendly answer should avoid hype and explain the specific problem where quantum methods may help.
aliases: quantum computing có thay PC không
